data.title: Disney CEO on $1 billion investment in OpenAI: ‘This is a good investment for the...
data.description:
Disney CEO Bob Iger and OpenAI CEO Sam Altman recently appeared on CNBC’s “Squawk on the Street” to discuss a significant $1 billion equity investment from the media giant into the artificial intelligence research and deployment company. This strategic move signals Disney’s commitment to leveraging cutting-edge AI technology to enhance its vast creative and business operations. The interview provided a window into the motivations behind this substantial investment and the potential implications for both companies and the broader media landscape.

Disney CEO Bob Iger spoke with CNBC’s ‘Squawk on the Street’ about the company’s recent $1 billion investment in OpenAI and the strategic rationale behind this significant financial commitment. He emphasized that the investment is not merely a financial transaction but a forward-looking strategy to integrate advanced AI capabilities into Disney’s core business. Iger articulated a clear vision: “We’ve been mindful of the significant growth in AI… extremely impressed with the progress that Sam and OpenAI have made… This is a good investment for the company.” This statement underscores a proactive approach to embracing technological evolution, recognizing AI’s transformative potential.

A core insight from the discussion revolves around Disney’s long-standing commitment to protecting its intellectual property. Iger highlighted Disney’s history of being aggressive in safeguarding its valuable assets. He noted their past interactions with other tech giants, including Google, where they have had to issue cease and desist letters due to perceived infringements or lack of respect for their IP. This historical context provides a crucial backdrop for understanding why the terms of the OpenAI deal, particularly its “partially exclusive” nature, are so important. It suggests a desire for a controlled and beneficial integration of AI that respects Disney’s creative legacy.

The investment is also framed as a strategic imperative for Disney to remain at the forefront of innovation in the entertainment industry. By partnering with OpenAI, a leader in generative AI, Disney aims to explore new avenues for storytelling, content creation, and operational efficiency. This could translate into more personalized experiences for consumers, enhanced animation techniques, and more sophisticated tools for content development. The partnership is not about replacing human creativity but augmenting it with powerful AI capabilities.

Sam Altman, CEO of OpenAI, echoed Iger’s sentiment, emphasizing the symbiotic nature of the partnership. He spoke about the profound potential of AI to unlock new forms of creativity and consumer engagement, aligning perfectly with Disney’s mission. The $1 billion investment is more than just capital; it represents a deep collaboration to explore how AI can revolutionize entertainment.

The “partially exclusive” nature of the licensing agreement, as mentioned by Iger, is a critical detail. While not fully exclusive, it suggests that Disney will have preferential access or rights to certain AI applications developed by OpenAI, particularly those relevant to its content and business. This strategic positioning allows Disney to harness the power of AI without ceding control of its core creative domains. It’s a calculated move to gain a competitive edge in an increasingly AI-driven world.

Ultimately, Disney’s substantial investment in OpenAI signifies a bold step towards integrating artificial intelligence into the heart of its entertainment empire. This partnership is poised to redefine how stories are told, experiences are crafted, and audiences are engaged, ensuring Disney’s continued relevance and leadership in the evolving media landscape.

data.title: Runware raises $50M to unify AI models with a single API
data.description:
The infrastructure layer beneath the current AI boom is getting expensive and fragmented. Runware, a startup aiming to simplify the entire deployment process, announced a $50 million Series A round today, led by Dawn Capital. Their pitch is simple but ambitious: provide one unified AI API for all models, promising massive performance gains and cost reductions for developers scaling intelligent applications.

Since its founding in 2023, Runware has quickly become a significant backbone, powering over 10 billion generations for hundreds of thousands of developers. The company argues that most teams deploying AI at scale face three major constraints: fragmented model access, slow inference that breaks user experience, and costs that increase faster than adoption.

Runware’s solution combines custom AI inference hardware with its proprietary Sonic Inference Engine, claiming up to a ten times lower pricing structure and faster performance than standard data center setups. This approach has already attracted major customers, including Wix, Quora, and Freepik, who rely on the platform to power image, video, and audio generation for millions of users.

The core of the offering is the unified AI API, which currently aggregates nearly 300 model classes and hundreds of thousands of model variants behind one consistent schema. This abstraction layer allows developers to A/B test, route, or swap models—whether open or closed source—with minimal code changes.

The economic argument is compelling: Runware promises 10x better price and performance for open source models and 10 to 40 percent savings even on closed source foundational models. For open source models specifically, they claim a 30 to 40 percent speed improvement compared with other inference platforms.

Decentralizing the AI Compute Stack

With the AI inference market projected to hit $70 billion by 2028, Runware is aggressively scaling its platform. A major goal is to deploy all two million-plus new AI models from Hugging Face onto the platform by the end of 2026, making every model available through that single API endpoint.

Crucially, they are also building out “inference PODs”—modular compute units that can be deployed rapidly (in weeks, not years) wherever power is cheap and available. This strategy bypasses traditional, slow data center buildouts, placing compute closer to the end-user and aligning with local regulatory needs.

As CTO Flaviu notes, they are building infrastructure for speed, cost-effectiveness, and redundancy. Ultimately, the goal, according to COO Ioana, is to give teams a single API that removes the need to juggle providers or commit to massive minimums, allowing developers to “focus on product.”

data.title: Disney and OpenAI reach three-year licensing agreement
data.description:
The financial world is abuzz with the news of a significant three-year licensing agreement between The Walt Disney Company and OpenAI, the artificial intelligence research and deployment company. This strategic alliance, announced on CNBC’s “Squawk on the Street,” signifies a substantial commitment from Disney, including a $1 billion equity investment in OpenAI and warrants for additional equity. The commentators highlighted the groundbreaking nature of this collaboration, emphasizing its potential to reshape the landscape of AI integration in media and entertainment.

This partnership positions Disney as a key player in leveraging advanced AI technologies. The agreement specifically allows Disney to utilize OpenAI’s Sora platform, a powerful tool capable of generating short, user-prompted videos featuring more than 200 characters. Furthermore, a curated selection of fan-inspired Sora videos will be made available on Disney+, broadening the platform’s content offerings and engaging audiences in novel ways. This strategic move signals Disney’s proactive approach to embracing generative AI for creative and commercial purposes.

The implications of this deal extend beyond content creation. Disney’s substantial investment underscores a recognition of AI’s transformative power and its potential to drive innovation across the entertainment industry. As one commentator noted, “This is where the technology is going.” This sentiment reflects a broader industry trend where established giants are actively seeking to integrate cutting-edge AI capabilities into their core operations and product development. The move also suggests a strategic pivot for Disney, aiming to harness AI to enhance its storytelling and user engagement strategies.

A crucial insight from the discussion is Disney’s intent to become a “major customer of OpenAI.” This implies a deep integration of OpenAI’s technology into Disney’s workflows, potentially spanning content generation, character animation, and personalized user experiences. The agreement to receive warrants for additional equity further solidifies Disney’s long-term interest and potential for growth alongside OpenAI, signaling a partnership built on shared vision and mutual benefit. This financial commitment also suggests a belief in OpenAI’s future trajectory and its capacity to deliver significant value.

The partnership also brings to light the ongoing competition and intellectual property considerations within the AI space. In a related development, it was reported that Disney has sent a cease-and-desist letter to Google. This letter alleges that Google has been using Disney’s copyrighted works without authorization to train and develop its generative AI models. This legal action highlights the critical importance of intellectual property rights in the rapidly evolving field of AI, particularly concerning the data used to train these sophisticated systems. The juxtaposition of these two events – the strategic partnership with OpenAI and the legal action against Google – paints a complex picture of Disney’s approach to AI, balancing collaboration with the protection of its valuable intellectual assets.

The agreement between Disney and OpenAI is a significant development, indicating a substantial shift in how major media companies are approaching artificial intelligence. By investing heavily and securing licensing rights, Disney is not merely experimenting with AI but is integrating it as a core component of its future strategy. This move will likely set a precedent for other media and entertainment companies, encouraging further exploration and adoption of generative AI technologies. The dual actions of forging a partnership and asserting intellectual property rights demonstrate a calculated strategy to navigate the opportunities and challenges presented by the AI revolution.

data.title: Steve Eisman on AI: LLM improvements will begin to gradually slow but not selling AI...
data.description:
“The big question on AI spending is, what kind of returns and cost savings the massive investments will bring.” This statement, from Steve Eisman, former senior portfolio manager at Neuberger Berman, encapsulates the core tension surrounding the current artificial intelligence boom. Eisman spoke with CNBC’s “Squawk Box” to discuss his perspective on the markets, particularly the burgeoning AI trade, and his own health.

Eiseman, known for his prescient short calls during the 2008 financial crisis, expressed a nuanced view on the current AI fervor. While acknowledging the transformative potential of AI, he cautioned against an overly optimistic outlook regarding the immediate profitability of large language models (LLMs). He posits that the rapid pace of improvement in LLMs is likely to decelerate. “We won’t know the answer for a while, and won’t be determined by change in depreciation schedules,” Eisman remarked, highlighting the uncertainty in quantifying the returns on these substantial investments.

A key insight from Eisman is his belief that the market is currently overemphasizing the immediate impact of AI. He observes that the debate is too focused on whether AI is a bubble, rather than on the fundamental question of how these technologies will actually deliver value. “I think what people are focusing too much on is the whole debate that’s going on is actually much more foundational,” he stated. This suggests a need for a more grounded assessment of AI’s practical applications and their eventual economic benefits.

Eisman also shared his personal investment strategy amidst this AI-driven market. Despite his reservations about the rapid growth trajectory of some AI stocks, he remains invested in many of them. “I own a lot of these stocks too. I own them all,” he admitted, indicating a balanced approach that acknowledges both the risks and the long-term potential. This stance reflects a pragmatic investor who is not abandoning the sector but is keenly aware of the need for careful evaluation.

He drew a parallel to the housing market crisis of 2008, where a foundational assumption proved to be flawed. In that instance, the assumption that housing prices would not decline proved to be incorrect, leading to a systemic collapse. Eisman suggests that a similar dynamic could unfold in the AI space if the assumption that LLMs will continue to scale indefinitely without encountering diminishing returns proves false. “Housing prices can’t go down. Once that assumption collapsed, the whole edifice collapsed,” he explained. This analogy serves as a stark warning about the potential consequences of unchecked optimism.

Furthermore, Eisman highlighted the work of individuals like Gary Marcus, a professor at NYU and a prominent critic of current AI development, who has been advocating for a more nuanced understanding of AI’s limitations. Marcus, who co-founded a company focused on AI, has consistently argued that LLMs, while impressive, are reaching a plateau in their development. Eisman seems to align with this perspective, suggesting that the rapid improvements seen thus far may indeed begin to slow. “Scaling LLMs as they keep scaling will start to lose their efficaciousness,” Eisman predicted. This implies that the current strategy of simply increasing model size and data may not yield proportional gains indefinitely.

The market’s current enthusiasm for AI, Eisman contends, is largely built on an assumption that has not yet been fully tested. He points to the fact that many companies are investing heavily in AI without a clear, quantifiable return on investment. This speculative fervor, he suggests, could be a precursor to a correction if the expected benefits do not materialize as anticipated. The critical question remains whether the immense capital being poured into AI will translate into tangible cost savings and productivity gains across industries.

Eisman’s commentary suggests that while AI is undeniably a powerful force, investors and technologists should temper their expectations regarding the speed and scale of its immediate impact. A more measured approach, focused on the fundamental economics and practical limitations of the technology, is likely to be more prudent.

data.title: Oracle’s AI Dip Signals Shifting Market, Not Fundamental Weakness
data.description:
The market’s immediate reaction to Oracle’s latest earnings report, a sharp downturn, belies a deeper narrative about the artificial intelligence landscape and broader economic sentiment. Stephanie Link, Chief Investment Strategist and Portfolio Manager at Hightower, spoke with CNBC’s Frank Holland, offering a nuanced perspective that challenges the knee-jerk pessimism, suggesting that while the AI trade might be momentarily losing favor, underlying strength and future opportunities abound.

Link’s commentary centered on the surprising market response to Oracle’s quarter, which she viewed as fundamentally robust. “I thought the quarter was actually quite good,” she stated, highlighting how earnings were in line with expectations and, crucially, Remaining Performance Obligations (RPOs) surged. This key metric, representing future contracted revenue, was “up 438% year-over-year, up 15% sequentially,” indicating substantial long-term demand for Oracle’s services. Furthermore, every segment of Oracle’s cloud business—total revenue, infrastructure, and applications—not only “beat expectations” but also “accelerated.”

The disconnect, Link explained, stemmed not from operational underperformance but from a lack of transparency regarding the financing mechanisms for Oracle’s significant capital expenditure. Investors sought greater clarity on how these large-scale investments would be funded. This absence of detail triggered a sell-off, even as the core business showed undeniable vigor in the AI-driven cloud space. Such a reaction underscores the market’s current sensitivity to financial specifics, especially in high-growth sectors where future commitments often outweigh present profitability in valuation models.

Despite the immediate market jitters, Link maintains a long-term bullish view on Oracle. She believes the current dip could represent a strategic entry point for investors. “At some point, Frank, this is going to be a buy,” she remarked, emphasizing the need to let the “dust settle” before making moves. This perspective is vital for founders and VCs who understand that market volatility often obscures intrinsic value, especially when the underlying demand for critical infrastructure like cloud and AI services remains robust. The market’s current valuation of Oracle at 33 times earnings might not be “cheap,” but the astounding RPO growth signals a powerful future trajectory.

This sentiment extends beyond Oracle to the broader AI ecosystem. Link observed, “I just think the AI trade right now is kind of getting out of favor.” This shift in investor preference, she argues, is temporary, suggesting that once the initial speculative froth dissipates, genuine opportunities for growth and investment will emerge. For tech insiders, this period of re-evaluation is critical, separating companies with solid fundamentals and clear long-term roadmaps from those riding purely on hype.

Her macroeconomic outlook reinforces this patient optimism. Link expressed “relief that we got through the Fed,” referring to the Federal Reserve’s recent policy decisions. She interprets the Fed’s commentary as signaling a healthy economy that is “growing better than expected” and likely to “accelerate into next year” due to increasing productivity. This positive economic backdrop provides a stable foundation for strategic investments, even as specific sectors like AI experience short-term recalibrations.

Beyond tech, Link identified the financial sector, particularly banks, as a compelling investment opportunity extending into 2026. This outlook is predicated on several factors: the potential for deregulation, the presence of “excess capital” within banks, and improving “net interest income and net interest margins.” Furthermore, accelerating fee income contributes to a healthier financial landscape.

She specifically cited Wells Fargo as an example, noting its strong consumer franchise and company-specific catalysts like the potential lifting of the asset cap, which would “increase growth initiatives and lower costs.” Her confidence in the financial sector also stems from a belief in the resilience of the economy’s upper tier. “The upper end is doing fine,” she asserted, acknowledging the long-standing “K-shaped” economic recovery where different segments perform unevenly. This sustained strength among higher-income consumers, coupled with favorable regulatory and financial conditions, positions banks well for future performance.

Ultimately, Link’s analysis provides a pragmatic lens through which to view current market dynamics. While the AI trade may be experiencing a momentary cooling, the underlying technological revolution and economic growth remain intact. For sophisticated investors and industry leaders, the current period demands discernment—identifying true value in companies like Oracle, which continue to demonstrate strong operational metrics despite market skepticism, and recognizing the broader economic currents that favor sectors like financials. The message is clear: caution is warranted, but opportunities are ripening for those who look beyond the immediate headlines.

data.title: Agentic Shopping Reshapes Retail: AI Agents Drive Purchases
data.description:
The retail landscape is undergoing a rapid, AI-driven transformation, moving beyond mere informational assistance to active transaction facilitation. What began as AI chat assistants providing basic product research has quickly evolved into a new paradigm: agentic shopping. This profound shift empowers AI to not just inform but actively facilitate and complete consumer purchases, fundamentally altering how users discover, evaluate, and acquire products across the digital ecosystem. This represents a significant leap from passive guidance to proactive engagement, demanding immediate attention from every player in the commerce sector.

Agentic shopping signifies a critical evolution in AI’s role within the commerce journey, transitioning from a supportive tool to an interactive agent. Previously, platforms like ChatGPT and Gemini primarily served as research aids, helping users compare products or gather specifications. Now, the latest iterations function as sophisticated shopping agents, capable of linking directly to products and even processing secure transactions without the user ever needing to leave the AI environment. This seamless integration streamlines the entire purchasing journey, from initial query and personalized recommendation to final checkout, creating an unprecedented level of convenience and immediacy for the consumer.

Consumer adoption of this advanced model is already demonstrating substantial momentum. According to the announcement, Salesforce research from November 2025 revealed that 25% of survey respondents had already made an AI-assisted purchase, with a striking 86% of those transactions completed by clicking a direct product link. The appeal is multifaceted and clear: shoppers overwhelmingly cite saving time, efficiently finding the best price, and conducting more thorough research as primary motivators for embracing AI-assisted purchases. This data underscores a strong consumer appetite for efficiency, optimization, and informed decision-making, capabilities that AI agents are uniquely positioned to deliver at scale. Furthermore, the prediction that 29% of non-users plan to adopt agentic shopping in 2026 signals a rapidly expanding market and a clear trajectory towards mainstream acceptance.

Navigating the Agentic Retail Landscape

For retailers, adapting to the rise of agentic shopping is no longer a future consideration; it is an immediate, strategic imperative that demands proactive engagement. The foundational step involves ensuring product data is not only readily accessible but also perfectly intelligible to the sophisticated algorithms of AI crawlers. This necessitates meticulously structuring product detail pages, leveraging advanced Schema Markup for semantic clarity, or establishing direct, real-time product feeds to AI chat assistants. Beyond technical infrastructure, a robust and nuanced content strategy is equally vital, focusing on natural language that authentically aligns with common user prompts and conversational queries, thereby significantly increasing the likelihood of products appearing prominently in AI-driven recommendations and purchase pathways.

The implications of agentic shopping extend far beyond mere technical adjustments, fundamentally redefining the traditional discovery and conversion funnel for brands. Retailers must now strategically compete for visibility within these emerging AI ecosystems, where the AI itself acts as a powerful, often opaque, intermediary between the brand and the consumer. This paradigm shift necessitates a comprehensive re-evaluation of existing digital marketing and SEO strategies, moving beyond conventional search engine optimization to actively optimize for AI agent interactions and recommendation logic. Brands that fail to adapt their data architecture and content strategy risk becoming effectively invisible in an increasingly AI-mediated marketplace, potentially ceding significant market share to more agile and technologically prepared competitors.

This evolution marks a profound and irreversible shift in the power dynamics between consumers, retailers, and the foundational AI platforms. Agentic shopping promises a future where personalized, hyper-efficient, and deeply integrated purchasing experiences become the expected norm, setting new benchmarks for customer convenience and brand engagement. Retailers must embrace this transformation with strategic foresight, investing not only in the necessary data infrastructure but also in continuous innovation of their content and customer engagement strategies to thrive in an era where AI agents are not just assistants, but active, influential participants in every transaction. The future of retail is undeniably agentic, demanding proactive engagement, adaptive strategies, and a commitment to continuous technological integration from all industry players.

data.title: Dissecting AI Agents: IBM’s Crume on Sensing, Thinking, and Acting
data.description:
Jeff Crume, PhD, a Distinguished Engineer at IBM, offers a precise and illuminating dissection of AI agents in his presentation, “Anatomy of AI Agents: Inside LLMs, RAG Systems, & Generative AI.” Crume’s core thesis centers on breaking down these intelligent systems into three fundamental, interconnected components: sensing, thinking, and acting. He illustrates how data from the real world is absorbed, processed into decisions, and subsequently translated into tangible actions, all while continuously learning and adapting.

The journey of an AI agent begins with “sensing,” its mechanism for perceiving the external environment. Crume explains that this perception can manifest in various forms. For a chatbot, it might be textual input, processed through natural language processing. For more complex systems like autonomous vehicles, it involves integrating data from a myriad of sensors, such as cameras and microphones. Additionally, agents can receive information through APIs and triggered events, acting as digital eyes and ears to gather necessary data for their operations.

Once information is sensed, it moves into the “thinking” phase, the cognitive core of the AI agent. This stage is significantly enhanced by incorporating external knowledge and predefined policies. Crume highlights the necessity of a “knowledge base” where the agent can access stored “facts, rules, and context,” drawing from sources like databases or Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG) systems. This external grounding prevents the agent from operating solely on its pre-trained data, offering up-to-date and domain-specific information.

Beyond knowledge, “policy information” is crucial. This includes “goals, objectives, [and] priorities” that guide the agent’s decision-making, ensuring its actions align with intended outcomes and operational boundaries. These policies establish the ethical and practical guardrails within which the agent must operate. The integration of both a rich knowledge base and clear policy directives ensures that AI agents are not only intelligent but also grounded and aligned with human intent.

The actual “thinking” process involves intricate reasoning. Crume describes this as employing “if-then-else kind of logic” to process incoming information and apply stored knowledge. Central to this is “planning” and “task decomposition,” where complex objectives are broken down into a sequence of smaller, actionable steps. Modern AI agents leverage sophisticated “machine learning” techniques for pattern recognition and “large language model technology” (LLM) for advanced reasoning, including chain-of-thought processes. These capabilities allow agents to understand complex queries, generate coherent plans, and make informed decisions.

The culmination of sensing and thinking is “acting.” This is where the AI agent translates its internal decisions into external manifestations. Actions can range from generating “text, speech, alerts,” or even “video” as outputs. More profoundly, agents can interact with digital systems by “read[ing] or writ[ing] to a database” or engaging with the physical world through “control” mechanisms, utilizing “actuators” in scenarios like robotics or self-driving cars. This ability to directly influence its environment underscores the transformative potential of AI agents.

A vital, often overlooked, component is the “feedback loop.” Crume emphasizes that agents must “constantly evaluat[e] its own performance.” This continuous self-assessment, frequently augmented by “reinforcement learning with human feedback” (RLHF), allows the agent to refine its understanding and actions. Whether it’s a simple “thumbs up or a thumbs down” from a user or self-correction through simulating alternative scenarios, feedback mechanisms are indispensable for an AI agent to learn, adapt, and improve over time, becoming more personalized and effective.

Consider the practical application of this anatomy in booking travel reservations. The agent “senses” dates and destinations, perhaps via a chatbot or by reading a calendar. Its “knowledge base” contains personal preferences (preferred airlines, hotel chains, desired locations for activities like running), alongside general data like maps, current prices, and availability from various travel APIs. “Policy” dictates spending caps or mandates preferred travel partners, ensuring adherence to corporate guidelines. The “thinking” module then processes all these inputs, planning an optimal itinerary. Finally, the agent “acts” by booking flights and hotels, delivering electronic tickets. A subsequent “survey” or direct feedback mechanism then informs the agent’s “thinking” module, allowing it to “keep tuning itself, keep getting better, keep getting smarter,” making future bookings even more tailored and efficient.

AI agents, as Crume meticulously outlines, are powerful orchestrators of perception, cognition, and action. They promise to enhance speed and efficiency across diverse domains, freeing human professionals from intricate, time-consuming details.

data.title: Enterprise AI: Beyond the Hype, Towards Tangible Value
data.description:
“The near-term is over-stated and the long-term is under-stated from an impact perspective,” observed Willem Avé, Global Head of Product at Square, encapsulating the prevailing sentiment around artificial intelligence in enterprise. This insight set a pragmatic tone for a Bloomberg Tech panel discussion at the Empowering Business Growth in the Digital Economy event in San Francisco, where Avé, alongside Nilesh Dusane of Amazon Web Services, Madhav Thattai of Salesforce, and Julie Gonzalez of Workday, engaged with Bloomberg’s AI Reporter Shirin Ghaffary on the transformative power of AI in everyday business operations. The conversation moved beyond the speculative “AI bubble” debate, delving into concrete applications and the nuanced challenges of integrating generative AI at scale.

A core insight emerging from the discussion was the critical distinction between traditional AI/machine learning and the more recent advent of generative AI. Nilesh Dusane, Global Head of Institutional Payments at Amazon Web Services, highlighted that while financial services have leveraged traditional AI/ML for years, generative AI, in its two to three years of prominence, is fundamentally changing the landscape in three key areas: productivity, risk management, and new value creation. For financial institutions, this translates to improved workflow automation for engineers, real-time decision-making for fraud mitigation through hyper-personalization, and the ability to build scalable, tailored customer experiences. This personalization is a significant leap, moving from generic alerts to in-context, specific messages that proactively identify unusual patterns and reduce losses.

For small businesses, often resource-constrained, AI offers a transformative agent. Willem Avé emphasized that these businesses are perpetually short on time. Providing them with AI-powered tools that offer insights or act as autonomous agents can be “game-changing.” He cited Square’s AI voice ordering product, which addresses post-COVID staffing shortages in restaurants, effectively acting as an “additional employee.” This underscores a second crucial insight: AI’s most profound impact often lies in augmenting human capabilities and filling critical gaps, rather than simply replacing existing roles.

However, the path to widespread enterprise AI adoption is not without its complexities. Madhav Thattai, SVP & COO of Agentforce Product at Salesforce, noted that while Salesforce has seen significant traction, with over half a billion in AI ARR and 18,000 customers moving from pilot to production, the use cases are still evolving. The initial focus is on straightforward tasks, with a gradual progression towards more complex autonomous actions. A paramount concern, especially in regulated sectors like finance, is the need for AI to be trustworthy, secure, and compliant, ensuring it doesn’t “hallucinate” but rather behaves consistently and accurately.

The integration of AI also necessitates a rethinking of existing processes and organizational structures. Julie Gonzalez, SVP of Financial Planning & Analysis at Workday, stressed that “interoperability is so core to how we’re thinking about agentic.” She highlighted the necessity of ensuring AI agents have the right data and context to make sound decisions, always with a “human in the loop” for critical people and money-related processes. Workday’s approach involves co-development with product teams, focusing on desired business outcomes rather than merely automating existing, potentially inefficient, processes. This means moving beyond making a process faster to fundamentally “unlearning what we’ve done over the past 20 years” and taking a completely different lens on how business outcomes should be achieved.

The hidden costs of scaling AI were also a significant point of discussion. Thattai identified three main challenges: the cost of trust, the cost of agent design, and the cost of continuous improvement and observability. Building trust requires ensuring AI experiences are reliable and accurate. The design of AI agents and their underlying architecture needs careful optimization to control their behavior and prevent inconsistencies. Moreover, as AI systems are deployed, the ability to measure their performance, analyze where they succeed or fail, and implement continuous improvements becomes crucial for managing ongoing costs.

The conversation also touched on the evolving workforce. Dusane shared an anecdote about a customer converting 10% of their call center agents into “prompt engineers,” signifying a cultural shift within the organization. This suggests that AI isn’t just about automation but also about creating new, higher-value roles that interact with and optimize AI systems. Gonzalez further elaborated that her team is looking to hire for “extremely curious” individuals who want to deeply understand the business, rather than solely focusing on traditional accounting qualifications like CPAs, as AI increasingly handles routine tasks.

The consensus was clear: generative AI is not a magic bullet, but a powerful tool that, when thoughtfully integrated with robust, purpose-built solutions and a human-centric approach, can unlock unprecedented value across industries. The journey requires a blend of technological innovation, strategic partnerships, and a willingness to adapt organizational culture and skill sets.

data.title: Brave AI Browsing Enters Testing, Redefining Web Interaction
data.description:
Brave has initiated early testing for its new AI browsing feature within its Nightly build, signaling a significant step towards an agentic web experience. This move aims to transform the browser into an intelligent partner, automating complex tasks and enhancing user productivity. However, this ambitious leap into AI-driven browsing inherently introduces substantial security and privacy challenges that Brave openly acknowledges.

The introduction of agentic AI into a browser environment is not without its perils. Giving an AI control over browsing activities could inadvertently expose personal data or lead to unintended actions, a risk Brave highlights as “inherently dangerous.” The company’s cautious rollout, confined to an opt-in feature flag in its Nightly channel, underscores the industry-wide struggle with securing AI-powered systems against sophisticated threats like indirect prompt injections. This measured approach reflects a necessary prudence given the potential for misuse or malfunction.

Brave’s strategy to mitigate these risks is multi-faceted, focusing on isolation and layered defenses. According to the announcement, AI browsing operates within a completely separate profile, ensuring that sensitive data from a user’s main browsing session, such as banking or email logins, remains inaccessible to the AI agent. This fundamental isolation is critical, acting as a primary firewall should other safeguards fail, preventing a compromised AI from reaching a user’s most private digital spaces.

The Intricacies of Agentic Security

Beyond isolation, Brave employs sophisticated model-based protections. A secondary “alignment checker” model scrutinizes the primary AI agent’s actions against the user’s original intent, acting as a crucial guardrail. This checker is deliberately firewalled from raw website content, reducing its susceptibility to page-level prompt injection attacks, a common vector for subverting AI models. Furthermore, Brave integrates models specifically trained to resist prompt injections, such as Claude Sonnet, alongside security-aware system instructions to guide the AI’s behavior.

User control and transparency remain central to Brave’s implementation. AI browsing must be manually invoked, and while the integrated AI assistant Leo can suggest actions, it cannot initiate agentic browsing without explicit user consent. The distinct visual styling of the AI browsing profile, akin to Brave’s Private Windows, provides clear cues to users about their operational mode. Users retain full ability to inspect, pause, or delete session data, ensuring they are always in command of the AI’s actions.

Brave’s commitment to privacy is also a cornerstone of this new feature. The company explicitly states that AI browsing never trains on user data, upholding its strict no-logs, no-retention policy. This stance differentiates Brave from many other AI service providers, reinforcing its privacy-first brand identity even as it ventures into advanced AI capabilities. The decision to avoid per-site permission prompts, instead reserving warnings for genuinely risky actions, reflects a pragmatic approach to user experience and security fatigue.

This early release of Brave AI browsing is more than just a new feature; it is a critical experiment for the entire agentic browser space. Brave is not merely building a product but actively contributing to the understanding of how to safely integrate powerful AI agents into a user’s most intimate digital environment. The insights gained from this testing phase, particularly regarding prompt injection defenses and user interaction, will undoubtedly inform future developments across the industry, shaping the trajectory of AI-powered web interaction for years to come.

data.title: AI’s Evolutionary March in Finance: Growth, Costs, and Data Readiness Define the Race
data.description:
Artificial intelligence is poised to boost sales and profits for financial institutions by up to 10% in the coming years, a compelling projection from Bloomberg Intelligence. This initial surge in revenue, however, comes hand-in-hand with significant upfront investments, highlighting an evolutionary rather than revolutionary path for AI adoption across the sector. The latest research presented by Diksha Gera, Senior Analyst, Global Payments & Fintech at Bloomberg Intelligence, underscores that while productivity gains are anticipated, the journey will be marked by strategic investment in capabilities, a realignment of workforce roles, and a heightened focus on data integrity and regulatory navigation.

Diksha Gera, Senior Analyst at Bloomberg Intelligence, presented findings from the firm’s latest research on AI trends shaping the future of finance at the Empowering Business Growth in the Digital Economy event in San Francisco. The comprehensive survey gathered insights from over 600 respondents, with 150 from financial services, including C-suite executives and senior directors across insurance, asset/wealth management, commercial banking, investment banking, and fintech/payments. The majority of these firms were large enterprises, with over 50% employing between 5,000 and 10,000 individuals, and 16% boasting a workforce exceeding 50,000. Geographically, North America accounted for 41% of respondents, and Europe for 50%.

The survey’s findings illuminate AI’s dual impact on financial services. Approximately 70% of financial institutions foresee a 1-10% increase in revenue growth over the next two to three years, with a similar percentage expecting a rise in profits. This suggests that the immediate benefits of AI are primarily revenue-driven, focusing on enhanced client engagement, personalization, and cross-selling opportunities. Yet, this growth isn’t without its costs.

Over 70% of respondents also anticipate higher operating costs due to AI deployment. This is largely attributed to substantial investments in data infrastructure, cloud migration, AI-related tools, and robust model governance. As Gera succinctly put it, “AI’s early phase in financial services is more likely to be about building capability rather than cost reduction.” This initial investment phase is critical for laying the groundwork for future efficiencies and profitability, with stronger incremental margin benefits expected beyond 2027-2028 as productivity gains fully materialize and investments stabilize.

Contrary to widespread fears of mass job displacement, AI is projected to fuel hiring within financial services. A significant two-thirds of financial services executives expect their headcount to rise over the next three years due to AI. More than half of these leaders plan to introduce new roles such as AI compliance specialists, workflow and agent designers, and model auditors. This signals a fundamental workflow realignment rather than a simple reduction in staff, emphasizing the creation of specialized positions to manage and leverage AI technologies.

The adoption curve for AI in financial services is notably more gradual compared to other sectors like retail or technology. Only 23% of financial services respondents report enterprise-wide scaling of AI tools, compared to 35% across all industries surveyed. This cautious approach stems from several inherent challenges: stringent regulatory and risk constraints, tight model governance, demands for explainability from regulators, fragmented legacy data systems, and strict oversight of personally identifiable information (PII). These factors collectively limit model training and add layers of cost and complexity to model development, slowing the pace of real-time deployment seen in less regulated industries.

Within the financial sector, payments companies are at the forefront of AI budget allocation, dedicating an average of 17% of their total technology spending to AI initiatives. Investment banking and wealth management follow closely. This aggressive investment highlights the potential for AI to transform commercial flows, an area ripe with inefficiencies. Mastercard, for instance, identifies an $80 trillion commercial flows opportunity, with $63 trillion in invoiced payments alone, much of which remains non-digital or involves significant manual intervention.

This landscape presents a unique opportunity for fintechs, particularly those addressing small and medium-sized businesses (SMBs). SMBs are the backbone of the global economy, yet they often grapple with manual, inefficient financial operations and significant labor challenges, including high labor costs and quality issues. AI is the first technology capable of interpreting unstructured workflows like invoices and emails, enabling end-to-end automation across accounts payable and receivable. This capability can significantly reduce operational costs, streamline processes, and enhance working capital cycles for SMBs.

For fintechs, leveraging AI in areas like merchant risk and underwriting can lead to lower loss rates and higher attachment rates for working capital. Platforms integrating AI into merchant-facing workflows can expand their competitive moats by offering streamlined onboarding, personalized recommendations, and efficient customer support. For example, PayPal has reported a 1.5x higher average margin per merchant and 2x more product activations through intent-driven onboarding, thanks to AI-powered solutions. In fraud detection, AI models are boosting detection rates by 20% to as much as 300%, blocking billions in fraudulent transactions annually. Square’s Managerbot, an AI assistant for its dashboard, exemplifies how AI can embed intelligence directly into merchant workflows, automating tasks and providing actionable insights for small businesses.

Ultimately, success in the AI era for financial services will be determined by data readiness, not merely by the sophistication of AI models. The survey confirms that data privacy and cybersecurity risks, along with the availability of clean data, are the top concerns for organizations. More than a quarter of participants identified data quality as their most pressing challenge, reflecting the industry’s reliance on accurate, well-governed datasets for model reliability and regulatory confidence. The winners will be those who master the “hard things”—organizing and securing their data—rather than solely focusing on the latest AI algorithms.
